title: Share of the population in poverty vs. Electoral Democracy Index
subtitle: Poverty is defined as living on less than $10 a day. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries. The electoral democracy index is by [V-Dem](#dod:v-dem), based on expert estimates, and ranges from 0 to 1 (most democratic).
caption:
Share of the population in poverty vs. Electoral Democracy Index, 2025

Poverty is defined as living on less than $10 a day. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries. The electoral democracy index is by V-Dem, based on expert estimates, and ranges from 0 to 1 (most democratic).

The scatter plot positions countries according to their share of the population living in poverty against their score on the Electoral Democracy Index. The vertical axis measures the poverty rate from 0% to 100%, while the horizontal axis tracks the democracy index from 0 to 1. A negative relationship is visible across the data points, where countries with lower democracy scores generally show higher poverty rates. In the upper-left quadrant, nations with low democracy scores and high poverty include Burundi, Ethiopia, and Laos, all positioned above the 80% poverty line. Other countries in this range include Bangladesh, Benin, and Kenya, which cluster between 80% and 90% poverty with democracy scores below 0.5. Moving toward the center, countries like Georgia, Armenia, and Indonesia show moderate poverty levels between 60% and 80% alongside mid-range democracy scores. In the lower-right section, countries with high democracy scores and low poverty rates include Australia, Chile, and Costa Rica, all falling below the 20% poverty mark with democracy scores exceeding 0.8. European nations such as Bulgaria and Moldova appear in the lower-middle section with poverty rates under 40% and democracy scores above 0.6. The data points form a distribution that slopes downward from left to right, with the highest poverty concentrations occurring in the least democratic regimes and the lowest poverty rates appearing in the most democratic ones.
